Infos zu bedenken: ABGABEDATUM: 12 Juni

Coding Conventions. • data sets are named with the prefix “d.” (e.g., d.cats). • linear model objects are named with the prefix “lm.” (e.g., lm.cats) • plots are named with the prefix “p.” • within the text R-objects are written using code formatting (e.g., lm.cats) 14 • variable names are written using italic (e.g., Body.weight) • these conventions are arbitrary, but hopefully useful to the reader

1 Introduction

1.1 Business Case

We propose a data-driven pricing decision-support system for Airbnb hosts in the Canton of Vaud, providing market-aligned price recommendations based on listing characteristics. The tool targets both new and existing hosts, using inputs such as location, room type, guest capacity, availability, ratings, and superhost status. Machine learning models, including LM, GLM, GAM, SVM, and Neural Networks, will be applied to identify key price drivers and generate accurate predictions. The objective is to balance revenue and occupancy, helping hosts understand their market value. A key feature is interpretability, enabling users to see why a certain price is recommended. From a business perspective, the system aims to increase booking rates, reduce price dispersion, and improve host satisfaction. The model is tailored to the Canton of Vaud to capture regional economic effects.

1.2 Research Questions

Several questions can be derived from the business case. These questions are intended to help us examine pricing dynamics and facilitate the development of a pricing strategy for Airbnb. First, we need to determine which factors influence guests’ willingness to pay. Furthermore, the nature of this influence is, of course, central to our analysis. Once these factors are identified, a second question immediately arises: to what extent can these factors be used to predict whether the prices of a rental property are justified?

Willingness to pay is actually the central variable here. However, it is not easy to measure, which is why a different approach must be taken. The approach we have chosen stems from the idea that the market generally functions well and that landlords intuitively already align with willingness to pay. As a result, the existing price for a rental property can also be assumed to be the price that tenants are willing to pay. Any deviations are offset in our subsequent calculations by the large number of observations. Thus, on average, the actual price will correspond to the price that tenants are willing to pay.

1.2.1 Hypothesis

H1: When host-centered indicators improve, tenants’ willingness to pay increases. H2: When location-centered indicators improve, tenants’ willingness to pay increases.

Host-centered indicators are those that relate to the host’s performance, such as ratings or Superhost status. In contrast, location-centered indicators are those that relate to factors such as the size of the rental property.

Note

Es sollte Möglich sein, die jeweiligen Modell in einer oder beiden Hypothesen zu verordnen. Sprich: ich untersuche mit meinem Modell H1 und finde dass mehr reviews den Preis steigen lassen blablabla. Zudem kann es in gewissen Modellen sinn machen, beide Hypothesen anzuschauen und zu beantworten. es kann aber auch sein, dass man zb H1 anschaut und mit Faktoren wie “accommodates” kontrolliert. dann habe ich zwar location-sided-Indicators, jedoch habe ich keinen Fokus auf die H2. Eine dritte Option ist, dass ich nicht eine der beiden Hypothesen untersuche, sondern die zweite Forshcungsfrage und versuche vorhersagen zu treffen. dies macht beispielsweise beim SVM sinn. da müsst ihr selbst enscheiden, welchen FOkus ihr setzen wollt

2 Data Preparation

2.1 Editing of Dataset

First step: Loading Packages

Second step: Reading all available Datasets

Cleaning the Airbnb-Listings Data. We keep only the variables needed for our project. We reduce the amount of variables to exact 20, according to the requirements by our lecturers.

According to the requirements for this paper, the dataset is limited to a maximum of 20 variables. To meet this specification, we have removed columns from the dataset that will not be used in our analysis. Consequently, d.listings_clean is our final, cleaned dataset, which we will use to run all subsequent models.

str(d.listings_clean)
'data.frame':   3876 obs. of  20 variables:
 $ host_response_time          : chr  "within an hour" "N/A" "within a few hours" "N/A" ...
 $ host_response_rate          : num  100 NA 100 NA 100 100 100 100 100 100 ...
 $ host_acceptance_rate        : num  100 NA 79 100 100 100 94 91 96 62 ...
 $ host_is_superhost           : logi  FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE TRUE TRUE ...
 $ host_verifications          : chr  "['email', 'phone']" "['email', 'phone']" "['email', 'phone']" "['email', 'phone']" ...
 $ host_has_profile_pic        : chr  "t" "t" "t" "t" ...
 $ host_identity_verified      : chr  "t" "t" "t" "t" ...
 $ neighbourhood_group_cleansed: chr  "Aigle" "Nyon" "Aigle" "Lausanne" ...
 $ latitude                    : num  46.4 46.5 46.3 46.5 46.5 ...
 $ longitude                   : num  7.13 6.33 7.06 6.64 6.8 ...
 $ property_type               : Factor w/ 63 levels "Barn","Boat",..: 18 44 18 16 52 18 18 44 11 18 ...
 $ room_type                   : Factor w/ 4 levels "Entire home/apt",..: 1 3 1 1 2 1 1 3 1 1 ...
 $ accommodates                : int  3 5 6 3 4 2 2 1 3 14 ...
 $ bathrooms                   : num  1 NA 3 1.5 1 1 1 1 1 2.5 ...
 $ beds                        : int  2 4 4 1 3 2 1 1 2 10 ...
 $ price                       : num  107 725 590 144 254 277 101 34 127 278 ...
 $ minimum_nights              : int  3 4 4 3 1 1 5 2 2 2 ...
 $ number_of_reviews           : int  70 0 18 22 85 125 26 292 41 197 ...
 $ estimated_occupancy_l365d   : int  42 0 0 0 42 120 0 78 150 78 ...
 $ review_scores_rating        : num  4.74 NA 4.94 4.68 4.75 4.87 4.23 4.67 4.95 4.68 ...
rm(d.airbnb_listings, d.raw_data_listings)

Subsequently, we calculated several additional features to include them as permanent columns in the dataset, ensuring they do not have to be recomputed for each analysis. These include: the log-transformed price—as price data is typically heavily right-skewed—the price per guest (total price divided by the number of guests accommodated), a boolean indicator for high demand, and a binary variable identifying whether the price exceeds the median.

3 Understanding the Data

Important to mention During our exploratory data analysis, we identified one ‘Private room’ listing with an unrealistically high price of 2’147’483’647 CHF. We excluded this single observation from our dataset as it is an extreme outlier that does not reflect the true market conditions and would negatively skew our predictive models.

# This keeps everything EXCEPT the row that is a Private room AND costs more than 5000
d.airbnb_listings_feat <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  filter(!(room_type == "Private room" & price > 100000))

in addition to the one outlier we wanted to remove one NA specifically

d.airbnb_listings_feat <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  filter(!host_has_profile_pic == "")
p1 <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = price)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "darkred", color = "white", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Raw Price", x = "Price", y = "Count")

p2 <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = log_price)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "darkgreen", color = "white", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Log Price", x = "Log Price", y = "Count")

p1 + p2

The histograms above clearly illustrate that the price distribution is heavily right-skewed. To satisfy the assumptions of our models and improve performance, we applied a log transformation. Post-transformation, the data exhibits a distribution that more closely resembles a Gaussian (normal) distribution.

p_acc <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = accommodates)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "dodgerblue", color = "white") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, max(d.airbnb_listings_feat$accommodates, na.rm = TRUE), by = 1)) +
  labs(title = "Listing Capacity", x = "Accommodates (Guests)", y = "Count")

p_rev <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = review_scores_rating)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "purple", color = "white", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Review Scores Rating Distribution", x = "Rating", y = "Count")

p_acc + p_rev

The listing capacity plot shows that most accommodations are designed for 2 or 4 guests. Regarding the review scores, a distinct trend is visible, with the vast majority of ratings falling between 4 and 5. Since both hosts and guests rate each other, we suspect this distribution may be influenced by a reciprocal rating bias (or leniency bias)

p_room <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = room_type, fill = room_type)) +
  geom_bar(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Proportion of Room Types", x = "Room Type", y = "Count") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

p_host <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat %>% filter(!is.na(host_is_superhost)), aes(x = as.factor(host_is_superhost), fill = as.factor(host_is_superhost))) +
  geom_bar(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("tomato", "seagreen")) +
  labs(title = "Superhost Status", x = "Is Superhost?", y = "Count")

p_room + p_host

The plots illustrate the distribution of room types and host statuses within the dataset. It is evident that the inventory is predominantly composed of Entire homes/apartments and Private rooms, while Hotel rooms and Shared rooms constitute a negligible minority. Furthermore, the data reveals that Superhost status is relatively uncommon; a significant majority of hosts do not hold this designation, with only approximately 30% of the sample categorized as Superhosts.

ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = room_type, y = log_price, fill = room_type)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Log Price by Room Type", x = "Room Type", y = "Log Price") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

top_neighborhoods <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>% 
  count(neighbourhood_group_cleansed, sort = TRUE) %>% 
  top_n(10, n) %>% 
  pull(neighbourhood_group_cleansed)

ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat %>% filter(neighbourhood_group_cleansed %in% top_neighborhoods), 
       aes(x = reorder(neighbourhood_group_cleansed, log_price, FUN = median), y = log_price)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Log Price by Top 10 Neighbourhood Groups", 
       x = "Neighbourhood Group", 
       y = "Log Price")

The boxplot “Log Price by Room Type” reveals significant differences in pricing structures across categories:

  • Entire homes/apartments and Private rooms: Both categories exhibit a large number of high-end outliers. In the context of the Swiss market, these likely represent luxury listings such as villas or mountain chalets. While private rooms show a similar outlier pattern, their median price remains significantly lower than that of entire homes.

  • Hotel rooms: Interestingly, hotel rooms show the highest median price. The distribution is much tighter with almost no outliers, suggesting a more standardized and professional pricing strategy compared to the highly variable peer-to-peer market.

  • Shared rooms: As expected, this category represents the most budget-friendly option with the lowest median and overall price range.

The second plot, “Log Price by Top 10 Neighbourhood Group”, highlights the impact of location on pricing:

There is a clear geographic disparity: regions like Riviera-Pays-d’Enhaut and Aigle command the highest median prices, whereas Gros-de-Vaud is the most affordable area.

Almost all top neighbourhoods show high-end outliers, confirming that luxury listings are spread across the canton, though the baseline price (the “floor”) varies significantly by location.

p_resp_time <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat %>% filter(host_response_time != "N/A" & !is.na(host_response_time)), aes(x = host_response_time)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "cadetblue") +
  labs(title = "Host Response Time", x = "Response Time", y = "Count") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

p_resp_rate <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = host_response_rate)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "cadetblue", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Host Response Rate", x = "Rate (0 to 1)", y = "Count")

p_acc_rate <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = host_acceptance_rate)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "cadetblue", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Host Acceptance Rate", x = "Rate (0 to 1)", y = "Count")

p_pic <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat %>% filter(!is.na(host_has_profile_pic)), aes(x = host_has_profile_pic)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "khaki4") +
  labs(title = "Has Profile Pic", x = "t = True, f = False", y = "Count")

p_verified <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat %>% filter(!is.na(host_identity_verified)), aes(x = host_identity_verified)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "khaki4") +
  labs(title = "Identity Verified", x = "t = True, f = False", y = "Count")

(p_resp_time | p_resp_rate | p_acc_rate) / (p_pic | p_verified)

The visualizations regarding host behavior and profile attributes reveal a high degree of professionalism and a strong intent to maximize booking opportunities:

Responsiveness: A vast majority of hosts respond “within an hour,” with the Host Response Rate peaking sharply at 100%. This indicates that hosts are highly motivated to engage with potential guests and secure bookings.

Reliability: Similarly, the Host Acceptance Rate is predominantly concentrated at 100%, suggesting that once a request is made, hosts are very likely to confirm the stay.

Trust and Verification: To establish credibility in a peer-to-peer marketplace, nearly all hosts utilize profile pictures and have completed identity verification. These “trust signals” are essential for reducing perceived risk for bookers and presenting the host as a reliable partner.

p_prop <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  count(property_type) %>%
  top_n(10, n) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(property_type, n), y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "coral") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Property Types (Count)", x = "Property Type", y = "Count")

p_neigh <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  count(neighbourhood_group_cleansed) %>%
  top_n(10, n) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(neighbourhood_group_cleansed, n), y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "coral3") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Neighbourhood Groups (Count)", x = "Neighbourhood", y = "Count")

(p_prop / p_neigh)

“To further understand the market composition, we examined the most frequent property types and their geographic distribution.

  • Property Types: The plot ‘Top 10 Property Types (Count)’ shows a massive dominance of ‘Entire rental units’, which account for nearly 2,000 listings. This is followed by ‘Private rooms in rental units’ and ‘Entire homes’. Specialized accommodations like chalets or villas are present but represent a much smaller share of the market.

  • Geographic Distribution: The chart ‘Top 10 Neighbourhood Groups (Count)’ highlights a strong regional concentration. Aigle and Lausanne are the most prominent hubs, with Aigle leading the count at over 1,000 listings. In contrast, regions like Gros-de-Vaud show significantly less activity in the top 10 ranking.”

p_baths <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = bathrooms)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.5, fill = "plum4", color = "white") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, max(d.airbnb_listings_feat$bathrooms, na.rm = TRUE), by = 1)) +
  labs(title = "Number of Bathrooms", x = "Bathrooms", y = "Count")

p_beds <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = beds)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "plum4", color = "white") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, max(d.airbnb_listings_feat$beds, na.rm = TRUE), by = 2)) +
  labs(title = "Number of Beds", x = "Beds", y = "Count")

p_baths | p_beds

The distribution plots for the number of bathrooms and beds reveal a strong concentration at lower values, as expected. However, the ‘Number of Beds’ plot in particular shows several extreme outliers, with a few listings claiming exceptionally high bed counts (up to over 40). While these values are not entirely implausible—they could represent large hostels or group dormitories—a qualitative verification was deemed impractical. Manually cross-referencing each of these extreme cases directly on the Airbnb website would be excessively time-consuming, hence they remain in the dataset as potential noise.

# Minimum Nights (Log scale due to extreme outliers like 365+ nights)
p_nights <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = minimum_nights)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "olivedrab", color = "white") +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "Minimum Nights $(Log_10$ Scale)", x = "Minimum Nights", y = "Count")

p_num_rev <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = number_of_reviews)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "olivedrab", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Total Number of Reviews", x = "Reviews", y = "Count")

p_occ <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = estimated_occupancy_l365d)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "olivedrab", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Estimated Occupancy", x = "Occupied Days", y = "Count")

p_nights | p_num_rev | p_occ

The histograms illustrate the distributions of booking requirements, guest engagement, and listing utilization.

Minimum Nights (\(Log_10\) Scale): To appropriately visualize this variable, a logarithmic transformation (base 10) was applied. This methodological choice was necessary due to severe right-skewness caused by extreme outliers, notably listings that enforce a 365-day minimum stay. Despite this transformation, the distribution clearly shows that the vast majority of hosts prefer short-term stays, with the highest frequencies concentrated around 1 to 3 nights.

Total Number of Reviews: The review count distribution is heavily right-skewed, indicating an L-shaped distribution. A large portion of listings has very few reviews, while a small minority of highly established listings accumulates a massive review volume. This suggests that the market is dominated by a few ‘super-performers’ alongside many newer or less frequently booked properties.

Estimated Occupancy: Similar to the review counts, the estimated occupancy shows a strong rightward skew. Most listings are occupied for only a small fraction of the year. However, a notable anomaly is the slight spike at the far right tail of the distribution. This peak likely represents highly commercialized listings or permanent rental units that operate at near full capacity throughout the year.”

# High Demand (Binary)
p_demand <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = as.factor(high_demand))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "sienna") +
  labs(title = "High Demand Classification", x = "0 = No, 1 = Yes", y = "Count")

# Price High (Binary)
p_price_high <- ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = as.factor(price_high))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "sienna") +
  labs(title = "High Price Classification", x = "0 = No, 1 = Yes", y = "Count")

(p_demand | p_price_high)

The bar charts illustrate the count of observations for our classification targets:

High Demand Classification: This variable exhibits a severe class imbalance. While the majority of listings are classified as “0” (low demand), only a small fraction meets the threshold for “1” (high demand). From a methodological standpoint, this imbalance suggests that Accuracy will be a misleading metric, as a model could achieve high accuracy by simply predicting the majority class. Consequently, we will prioritize the F1-Score and Precision-Recall curves to evaluate model performance for this target.

High Price Classification: In contrast, the distribution for high price is nearly perfectly balanced. This is the intended result of our decision to split the data using the median price. Since exactly half of the listings fall above and half below this value, the classes are equally represented. This provides an ideal baseline for classification algorithms, as the models will not be inherently biased toward a majority class.

d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  count(host_verifications) %>%
  top_n(5, n) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(host_verifications, n), y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "grey50") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 5 Host Verification Combinations", x = "Verification Methods", y = "Count")

To complete the profile of host credibility, we examined the specific combinations of verification methods used. The chart “Top 5 Host Verification Combinations” reveals a highly standardized approach to trust within the platform:

  • Platform Standards: The vast majority of hosts rely on the dual verification of email and phone. This combination represents the baseline requirement for establishing a functional and reachable profile on Airbnb.

  • Professionalism Indicator: A smaller but notable segment includes a “work_email” in their verification set. This often serves as a proxy for professional or commercial hosts who manage listings as part of a business entity rather than as private individuals.

  • Minimalist Profiles: Profiles with only one or no verification methods are extremely rare, suggesting that the community (and likely the platform’s own ranking algorithms) enforces a minimum level of identity disclosure to maintain marketplace integrity.

4 Data Quality

To ensure the integrity of our analysis, we performed a systematic check for missing values (NAs) within the dataset. We utilized the naniar and visdat packages, which allow for both a numerical summary and a spatial visualization of missingness patterns.

Visualization and Identification: Using vis_dat() and gg_miss_var(), we identified that the missing data is not uniformly distributed but concentrated in specific features.

Response and Acceptance Rates: A high volume of NAs was observed in the “Rate” columns (e.g., host response and acceptance rates). An initial hypothesis suggested that these missing values might represent zero values that were not correctly captured during the data import process. While imputing these as 0% was considered, we proceeded with caution to avoid distorting host performance metrics.

Review Ratings and Methodological Decision: For the review score variables, we determined that the data is likely Missing Not At Random (MNAR). A listing without reviews often indicates it is new or has not yet been booked, meaning a “rating” simply does not exist for that observation.

Final Handling: We decided to omit observations with missing ratings from our models rather than imputing them. Imputing a zero would incorrectly categorize these listings as “poorly rated,” while imputing a mean would artificially reduce the variance of our predictors. By omitting these cases, we ensure that our model training is based on verified, existing performance data.

vis_miss(d.airbnb_listings_feat)

gg_miss_var(d.airbnb_listings_feat)

gg_miss_upset(d.airbnb_listings_feat)

### Aus Understanding the Data

# Calculate percentage of missing values per column
missing_data <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ mean(is.na(.)) * 100)) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Missing_Percentage") %>%
  filter(Missing_Percentage > 0) %>%
  arrange(desc(Missing_Percentage))

# Plot missing values
ggplot(missing_data, aes(x = reorder(Variable, Missing_Percentage), y = Missing_Percentage)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Percentage of Missing Values per Variable",
       x = "Variables",
       y = "% Missing")

### Missing Values (NA) Check

This step is less for the client and essential for your ML models. Most modeling functions (like lm() or svm()) will throw errors or silently drop entire rows if NAs are present.

# Count the number of NAs for each key column in your dataset
na_summary <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  select(price, log_price, accommodates, number_of_reviews, host_is_superhost) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) %>%
  t() %>% # Transpose for better readability
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rename(Missing_Values = V1)

kable(na_summary, caption = "Count of Missing Values per Variable")
Count of Missing Values per Variable
Missing_Values
price 0
log_price 0
accommodates 0
number_of_reviews 0
host_is_superhost 0

5 EDA (DAVIDE)

# --- 5. Binary Predictor: Superhost Status ---
# Adding this because it's a key part of your business case and fulfills the binary variable requirement.
ggplot(data = d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = as.factor(host_is_superhost), y = log_price, fill = as.factor(host_is_superhost))) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("0" = "Regular Host", "1" = "Superhost")) +
  labs(
    title = "Price Difference: Superhosts vs. Regular Hosts",
    x = "Host Status",
    y = "Log(Price)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# --- 6. Spatial Data: Mapping the Listings ---
# Cleaned up the map to focus purely on location and price.
ggplot(data = d.airbnb_listings_feat, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = log_price), alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  labs(
    title = "Airbnb Listings in Vaud by Price",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude",
    color = "Log(Price)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

5.1 Davide’s overall Data Analysis

5.1.1 1. Overall Market Overview (Prices & Capacity)

This is the absolute minimum, maximum, median, and mean for the most important numeric variables.

# Basic summary statistics for key numerical variables
summary_stats <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  select(price, log_price, accommodates, number_of_reviews) %>%
  summary()

print(summary_stats)
     price          log_price      accommodates    number_of_reviews
 Min.   :  20.0   Min.   :2.996   Min.   : 1.000   Min.   :  0.00   
 1st Qu.:  80.0   1st Qu.:4.382   1st Qu.: 2.000   1st Qu.:  1.00   
 Median : 120.0   Median :4.787   Median : 3.000   Median :  7.00   
 Mean   : 178.7   Mean   :4.858   Mean   : 3.755   Mean   : 29.53   
 3rd Qu.: 185.0   3rd Qu.:5.220   3rd Qu.: 5.000   3rd Qu.: 32.00   
 Max.   :9342.0   Max.   :9.142   Max.   :16.000   Max.   :637.00

Client Interpretation: “The average Airbnb in Vaud costs X CHF per night, but the median is Y CHF, indicating that a few extremely expensive luxury listings might be skewing the average upward. Prices range from a minimum of A CHF to a maximum of B CHF.”

5.1.2 2. Analysis by Room Type

Since we want to know how many different types of rooms there are, we group the data and calculate the counts, percentages, and price ranges for each type.

# Detailed breakdown by Room Type
room_type_analysis <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  group_by(room_type) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Listings = n(),
    Percentage_of_Market = round((n() / nrow(d.airbnb_listings_feat)) * 100, 1),
    Min_Price = min(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    Median_Price = median(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    Average_Price = round(mean(price, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    Max_Price = max(price, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Total_Listings)) # Sorts by the most common room type

print(room_type_analysis)
# A tibble: 4 × 7
  room_type       Total_Listings Percentage_of_Market Min_Price Median_Price
  <fct>                    <int>                <dbl>     <dbl>        <dbl>
1 Entire home/apt           2907                 75          24        141  
2 Private room               940                 24.3        20         67.5
3 Shared room                 19                  0.5        23         47  
4 Hotel room                   8                  0.2        54        264. 
# ℹ 2 more variables: Average_Price <dbl>, Max_Price <dbl>

Client Interpretation: “Entire homes/apartments dominate the Vaud market, making up 75% of all listings. As expected, the Hotel room command the highest median price (264.5 CHF), compared to shared rooms (47 CHF).”

5.1.3 3. Superhost vs. Regular Host Breakdown

This business case specifically mentions superhost status. So we took a look to see how many there are and if they actually charge more or even less.

# Superhost statistics
superhost_analysis <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  group_by(host_is_superhost) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Hosts = n(),
    Percentage = round((n() / nrow(d.airbnb_listings_feat)) * 100, 1),
    Median_Price = median(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_Reviews = round(mean(number_of_reviews, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
  ) %>%
  mutate(host_is_superhost = ifelse(host_is_superhost == 1, "Yes", "No"))

print(superhost_analysis)
# A tibble: 2 × 5
  host_is_superhost Total_Hosts Percentage Median_Price Avg_Reviews
  <chr>                   <int>      <dbl>        <dbl>       <dbl>
1 No                       2805       72.4          122        17.3
2 Yes                      1069       27.6          112        61.6

5.1.4 1. Correlation Matrix (Crucial before Machine Learning)

Before we train our LM, GAM, or SVM, we need to know how our numerical variables relate to each other. This helps us identify multicollinearity (when two predictors provide the same information) and strong price drivers.

# Select relevant numerical variables (drop NAs just for the correlation calculation)
cor_data <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  select(log_price, accommodates, number_of_reviews) %>%
  drop_na()

# Calculate the correlation matrix
cor_matrix <- cor(cor_data)

# Render a nice table for your RMarkdown document
kable(round(cor_matrix, 2), caption = "Correlation Matrix of Key Numerical Drivers")
Correlation Matrix of Key Numerical Drivers
log_price accommodates number_of_reviews
log_price 1.00 0.65 -0.16
accommodates 0.65 1.00 -0.04
number_of_reviews -0.16 -0.04 1.00

Client Interpretation: “As expected, accommodates shows the strongest positive correlation with price. It is also interesting to note the relationship between availability and number_of_reviews…”

5.1.5 2. Detailed property_type Analysis

We already analyzed room_type, but Airbnb often has dozens of property_types (e.g., “Entire chalet”, “Private room in castle”, etc.). Summarizing the Top 10 property types in the Canton of Vaud provides maybe a great market insight.

# Top 10 Property Types by frequency and their median price
top_properties <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  group_by(property_type) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Listings = n(),
    Median_Price = median(price, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Total_Listings)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10) # Keep only the top 10

kable(top_properties, caption = "Top 10 Property Types in the Canton of Vaud")
Top 10 Property Types in the Canton of Vaud
property_type Total_Listings Median_Price
Entire rental unit 1973 128.0
Private room in rental unit 409 64.0
Entire home 287 250.0
Entire chalet 219 250.0
Private room in bed and breakfast 194 69.5
Entire condo 184 136.5
Private room in home 154 66.0
Entire villa 50 370.0
Private room in villa 45 68.0
Room in hotel 40 177.0

Client Interpretation: “While standard apartments like”entire rental unit” are the most common, specialty properties like chalets, homes and villas command a significant premium, which our model will need to capture.”

6 LM (TIMON - Davide translated to english and commments)

6.1 Theory

With this initial model, we aim to examine the first hypothesis and investigate which host-centered indicators influence the price of a rental property.

For this, we use the review_scores_rating. We also include the factor host_is_superhost to distinguish between the two groups. We measure willingness to pay here using the effective price of the rental property. Additionally, we introduce two control variables. First, the size of the rental property, as this obviously influences and skews the rental price, and second, the type of room. This also significantly influences the price.

Since there is a high risk of multicollinearity in our data, this is also examined.

Hier muss noch ein Teil rein bezüglich den Vorgaben des Models und inwiefern diese erfüllt sind.

6.2 calculating Models

# Fitting a Multiple Linear Regression model to predict the log-transformed price.
# We use log(price) to account for the right-skewed distribution of Airbnb prices.
lm.main <- lm(log(price) ~ 
                review_scores_rating + 
                as.factor(host_is_superhost) +
                host_has_profile_pic + 
                accommodates + 
                as.factor(room_type), 
              data = d.airbnb_listings_feat)

summary(lm.main)

Call:
lm(formula = log(price) ~ review_scores_rating + as.factor(host_is_superhost) + 
    host_has_profile_pic + accommodates + as.factor(room_type), 
    data = d.airbnb_listings_feat)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-2.7063 -0.2669 -0.0329  0.2443  2.8498 

Coefficients:
                                  Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                       4.021568   0.108881  36.936  < 2e-16 ***
review_scores_rating              0.071481   0.021446   3.333 0.000869 ***
as.factor(host_is_superhost)TRUE -0.113674   0.017119  -6.640 3.67e-11 ***
host_has_profile_pict             0.040513   0.040557   0.999 0.317921    
accommodates                      0.149491   0.003626  41.230  < 2e-16 ***
as.factor(room_type)Hotel room    0.559211   0.154972   3.608 0.000313 ***
as.factor(room_type)Private room -0.408747   0.020409 -20.027  < 2e-16 ***
as.factor(room_type)Shared room  -0.817994   0.109874  -7.445 1.25e-13 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.437 on 3152 degrees of freedom
  (714 Beobachtungen als fehlend gelöscht)
Multiple R-squared:  0.5384,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.5373 
F-statistic: 525.1 on 7 and 3152 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
# Checking for multicollinearity using Variance Inflation Factor (VIF).
# Values range from 1 to infinity. A value of exactly 1 indicates no collinearity.
# Values > 5-10 suggest problematic multicollinearity among the predictor variables.
multicollinearity(lm.main)

6.2.1 LM Controlled Plots (TIMON - Davide translated to english and commments)

# --- LM controlled plots ---
# Visualizing the raw relationship between review scores, log(price), and superhost status.
ggplot(data = d.airbnb_listings_feat, mapping = aes(review_scores_rating, log(price), color = host_is_superhost)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

# Creating a grid of hypothetical data to predict and plot the fitted LM lines cleanly.
# We hold 'room_type' and 'accommodates' constant to isolate the specific effects of reviews and superhost status.
d.lm_predict <- expand.grid(
  review_scores_rating = seq(3, 5, length.out = 100),
  host_is_superhost = c("TRUE", "FALSE"),
  room_type = "Entire home/apt",   # Fixed reference category
  host_has_profile_pic = c("t", "f"),
  accommodates = mean(d.airbnb_listings_feat$accommodates, na.rm = TRUE) # Fixed at mean value
)

# Generating predictions based on the trained LM model
d.lm_predict$pred <- predict(lm.main, newdata = d.lm_predict)

# Plotting the actual data points alongside our model's predicted regression lines
ggplot(d.airbnb_listings_feat, 
       aes(review_scores_rating, log(price), color = host_is_superhost)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(data = d.lm_predict, aes(y = pred), size = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "LM Predictions: Log(Price) vs. Review Rating",
       x = "Review Scores Rating",
       y = "Log(Price)")

6.3 Interpretation

The model shows that both the hosts’ rating and their Superhost status have a significant influence. The rating has a positive effect of 0.071. In contrast, Superhost status has a negative effect of -0.114. The assumption that tenants are willing to pay more when renting from a Superhost therefore does not necessarily seem to hold true. One possible explanation for this phenomenon is that Superhosts may have a better understanding of the market because they have been active for a longer time, and therefore set lower prices in order to achieve a higher occupancy rate. This is relevant for Airbnb, as these results show that reviews on the host side are a key factor in price setting. It is also interesting that Superhosts apparently develop their own understanding for how prices can be set. The exact reasons underlying this difference cannot be examined within the scope of this paper. However, these findings provide Airbnb with clarified insights that can serve as a basis for further work.

7 GLM Poisson (PASCAL - Davide translated to english and commments)

7.1 Theory

With the GLM model with Poisson-family / quasi-poisson family is used to analyse count data. Count data is strictly positive and counted (not continuous). Using this model we want to see which variables lead to the highest occupancy, meaning most of the time the airbnb is rented out. As the data is probably overdispersed - meaning the data has not the same variance and much more cheap listings than expensive ones - we need to use a quasi-poisson model that can handle this kind of data much better. First we fit a normal poisson-family, then check for overdispersion, and if necessary we use a quasi-poisson-family.

7.2 Model

# --- GLM set to Poisson (Pascal) ---

glm_poisson <- glm(estimated_occupancy_l365d ~ price + host_is_superhost + 
                     review_scores_rating + accommodates + room_type, 
                   data = d.airbnb_listings_feat, 
                   family = "poisson")

summary(glm_poisson)

Call:
glm(formula = estimated_occupancy_l365d ~ price + host_is_superhost + 
    review_scores_rating + accommodates + room_type, family = "poisson", 
    data = d.airbnb_listings_feat)

Coefficients:
                        Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)            2.803e+00  4.325e-02  64.817  < 2e-16 ***
price                 -3.065e-03  3.744e-05 -81.858  < 2e-16 ***
host_is_superhostTRUE  9.571e-01  5.062e-03 189.086  < 2e-16 ***
review_scores_rating   2.264e-01  9.143e-03  24.766  < 2e-16 ***
accommodates           3.835e-02  1.433e-03  26.763  < 2e-16 ***
room_typeHotel room    5.528e-01  3.811e-02  14.504  < 2e-16 ***
room_typePrivate room -1.364e-01  6.300e-03 -21.649  < 2e-16 ***
room_typeShared room  -1.884e-01  3.530e-02  -5.338 9.38e-08 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for poisson family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 233454  on 3159  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 178175  on 3152  degrees of freedom
  (714 Beobachtungen als fehlend gelöscht)
AIC: 192199

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6
# Checking for Overdispersion.
dispersion_ratio <- glm_poisson$deviance / glm_poisson$df.residual
print(paste("Dispersion Ratio:", dispersion_ratio))
[1] "Dispersion Ratio: 56.5276182322456"
glm_quasipoisson <- glm(estimated_occupancy_l365d ~ price + host_is_superhost + 
                          review_scores_rating + accommodates + room_type, 
                        data = d.airbnb_listings_feat, 
                        family = "quasipoisson")

summary(glm_quasipoisson)

Call:
glm(formula = estimated_occupancy_l365d ~ price + host_is_superhost + 
    review_scores_rating + accommodates + room_type, family = "quasipoisson", 
    data = d.airbnb_listings_feat)

Coefficients:
                        Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)            2.8032800  0.3668065   7.642 2.81e-14 ***
price                 -0.0030647  0.0003175  -9.652  < 2e-16 ***
host_is_superhostTRUE  0.9571456  0.0429320  22.294  < 2e-16 ***
review_scores_rating   0.2264382  0.0775468   2.920  0.00352 ** 
accommodates           0.0383450  0.0121514   3.156  0.00162 ** 
room_typeHotel room    0.5528174  0.3232586   1.710  0.08734 .  
room_typePrivate room -0.1363942  0.0534351  -2.553  0.01074 *  
room_typeShared room  -0.1884285  0.2993678  -0.629  0.52912    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for quasipoisson family taken to be 71.93213)

    Null deviance: 233454  on 3159  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 178175  on 3152  degrees of freedom
  (714 Beobachtungen als fehlend gelöscht)
AIC: NA

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6
AIC(glm_quasipoisson)
[1] NA

7.3 Crossvalidation

set.seed(1)

cv.mse.poisson <- c()
cv.mse.quasipoisson <- c()

for(i in 1:200){
  #dataprep
  n <- nrow(d.airbnb_listings_feat)
  cv.ind_permuted <- sample(1:n, replace = FALSE)
  d.airbnb_listings_feat_permuted <- d.airbnb_listings_feat[cv.ind_permuted,]
  
  #create folds
  cv.folds <- cut(1:n, breaks = 3, labels = FALSE)
  cv.ind_test <- which(cv.folds == 1)
  
  d.cv_test <- d.airbnb_listings_feat_permuted[cv.ind_test, ]
  d.cv_train <- d.airbnb_listings_feat_permuted[-cv.ind_test, ]
  
  #train model poisson
  cv.glm_poisson_train <- glm(formula = formula(glm_poisson), 
                          data = d.cv_train)
  cv.glm_poisson_predict <- predict(cv.glm_poisson_train, newdata = d.cv_test)
  
  cv.mse.poisson[i] <- mean((d.cv_test$estimated_occupancy_l365d - cv.glm_poisson_predict)^2, 
                           na.rm = TRUE)
  
  
  #train model quasipoisson
  cv.glm_quasipoisson_train <- glm(formula = formula(glm_quasipoisson), 
                          data = d.cv_train)
  cv.glm_quasipoisson_predict <- predict(cv.glm_quasipoisson_train, newdata = d.cv_test)
  
  cv.mse.quasipoisson[i] <- mean((d.cv_test$estimated_occupancy_l365d - cv.glm_quasipoisson_predict)^2, 
                           na.rm = TRUE)
  
}

boxplot(cv.mse.poisson, cv.mse.quasipoisson, names = c("Poisson-Model","Quasipoisson-Model"))

Interpretation: die beiden Modelle unterscheiden sich nicht. wir erhalten keine outlier in den vorhersagen sprich das Model ist sehr stabil.

7.4 Interpretation GLM-Poisson:

As expected, the GLM with a Poisson distribution produced overdispersion, since the variability in the Airbnb data was to be expected. The model calculated the standard errors too small, which is why all variables were highly significant in the first output. The dispersion was then calculated and found to be greater than 1, specifically 56.53. A quasi-Poisson model was used as a solution, which adjusts the standard errors upward.

7.5 Interpretation GLM-Quasipoisson

The GLM with quasi-Poisson distribution has corrected the overdispersion. The standard errors were adjusted upwards (significantly) and now provide a realistic picture of which factors truly have an influence on occupancy. Interpretations: - Price -> highly significant negative. As expected: The higher the price, the lower the occupancy - Superhost: highly significant positive: Attention, logged rates, therefore calculate the effect with the exponential function exp(0.9577) = 2.6. Being a Superhost increases occupancy by 2.6 times compared to a normal host. - Reviews: Positive and significant. Better reviews lead to more bookings. - Capacity: Positive and significant. The more people that fit, the more bookings. - Room types: In the intercept is “Entire home/apt”. Private Room is booked significantly worse than the entire accommodation. Shared Room has a high p-value. Statistically, it cannot be said whether the occupancy of a shared room differs from the entire apartment.

8 GLM Binomial (DAVIDE)

8.1 Theory

8.2 Model

Feature Engeneering

# --- GLM Binomial (DAVIDE) ---
# Fitting a Logistic Regression (Binomial GLM) to predict if a listing is priced above the market median.
# The target variable 'price_high' is binary (1 = above median price, 0 = below median price).
glm_binomial <- glm(price_high ~ host_is_superhost,
                    data   = d.airbnb_listings_feat,
                    family = binomial(link = "logit"))

summary(glm_binomial)

Call:
glm(formula = price_high ~ host_is_superhost, family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
    data = d.airbnb_listings_feat)

Coefficients:
                      Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
(Intercept)            0.01783    0.03776   0.472  0.63691   
host_is_superhostTRUE -0.22247    0.07216  -3.083  0.00205 **
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 5368.7  on 3873  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 5359.1  on 3872  degrees of freedom
AIC: 5363.1

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 3

FEEDBACK TIMON:

  • eher simpel, kann man hier allenfalls mindestens noch auf accommodates kontrollieren? ich bin nicht ganz sicher ob man das inerhalb eines solchen glms darf, aber eigentlich ist eine einzige UV schon zu wenig.

8.3 Interpretation GLM Binomial: Calculating Odds Ratios (DAVIDE)

Similar to the Poisson model, the coefficients in a binomial model with a logit link are log-odds.

# --- Interpretation GLM Binomial: Calculating Odds Ratios ---
# Similar to the Poisson model, the coefficients in a binomial model with a logit link are log-odds.
# We exponentiate the estimates (exp) to convert them into easily interpretable Odds Ratios.
d.results_glm_binomial <- tidy(glm_binomial) %>%
  mutate(
    odds_ratio = round(exp(estimate), 3),
    p.value    = round(p.value, 4)
  ) %>%
  select(term, estimate, odds_ratio, p.value)

print(d.results_glm_binomial)
# A tibble: 2 × 4
  term                  estimate odds_ratio p.value
  <chr>                    <dbl>      <dbl>   <dbl>
1 (Intercept)             0.0178      1.02    0.637
2 host_is_superhostTRUE  -0.222       0.801   0.002

FEEDBACK TIMON Soweit keine beanstandungen.sieht gut aus. Chatgpt hat noch vorgeschlagen allenfalls ein Konfidenzintervall zu rechnen für diese Werte, aber ich weiss nicht ob das in diesem Rahmen nötig sein würde. -> eine ARt der Visualisierung wäre für dieses Model sicher auchnoch wichtig.

What the Output Tells Us: The coefficient for host_is_superhost is negative (-0.2217) and highly significant (p = 0.002). The calculated Odds Ratio of 0.801 means that Superhosts have roughly 20% lower odds of pricing their listings above the median market price compared to regular hosts. Consequently, Superhosts tend to be cheaper, not more expensive.

At first glance, this seems to contradict our Hypothesis 3 (H3: “If a host is a superhost, the WTP of a guest increases / they charge more”). However, the real business insight emerges when we combine this finding with Pascal’s Quasi-Poisson model.

The Superhost Strategy: Volume over Margin Pascal’s model demonstrated that having Superhost status increases occupancy by a factor of 2.6. The Binomial GLM shows that Superhosts are more likely to price below the median. Together, this creates a coherent picture of the Superhost business strategy:

  • Superhost = Lower Price + Significantly Higher Occupancy

  • Regular Host = Higher Price + Significantly Lower Bookings

Superhosts actively optimize for volume rather than margin. This makes perfect business sense: to achieve and maintain Superhost status on Airbnb, hosts need a high volume of bookings and a consistent stream of positive reviews. This is much easier to achieve with highly competitive pricing.

Therefore, while H3 is not confirmed in the sense that “Superhosts charge a premium,” the models reveal the opposite, which is a much stronger strategic finding: Superhosts price strategically lower but achieve massively more bookings as a result.

Recommendation for Hosts: A host aspiring to reach Superhost status should not increase their prices. Instead, they should use competitive pricing to gather bookings and reviews. The financial reward of the Superhost status pays off indirectly through high occupancy rates, rather than a higher nightly single-booking price.

9 GAM Model (DAVIDE)

9.1 Theory

9.1.1 Why GAM for Airbnb Pricing?

Generalized Additive Models (GAM) extend the classical linear model by replacing rigid linear coefficients with smooth, non-parametric functions f(x). The model takes the form:

\[g(E[Y]) = β₀ + f₁(x₁) + f₂(x₂) + ... + fₚ(xₚ)\]

where \(g(·)\) is a link function and each \(fⱼ(·)\) is a smoothing spline whose shape is learned from the data rather than pre-specified.

9.1.2 Justification for our Use Case

For Airbnb pricing in the Canton of Vaud, GAM offers three decisive advantages over a standard linear model:

1. Non-linear price effects are the rule, not the exception.
The relationship between guest capacity (accommodates) and price is not linear: going from 2 to 4 guests typically doubles the price, but going from 8 to 10 guests adds proportionally less. Likewise, review_scores_rating shows threshold effects — listings below 4.0 are penalised disproportionately, while differences between 4.8 and 5.0 matter less. A linear model would force an “average slope” through these curves and misprice both ends of the spectrum.

2. Spatial pricing is inherently non-linear.
The Canton of Vaud combines a high-priced lakeshore (Lausanne, Montreux, Vevey), urban centres, alpine resorts (Villars, Leysin), and rural hinterland — each with distinct price regimes. A 2D smoother s(longitude, latitude) captures this price surface without requiring us to manually engineer postcode dummies or distance-to-lake variables. This is the single most important methodological argument for choosing GAM in a regional pricing tool.

3. Interpretability is preserved.
Unlike black-box models (Random Forests, Neural Networks), each smooth term \(fⱼ(xⱼ)\) can be plotted and interpreted individually. This matches our business case requirement that hosts must be able to see why a certain price is recommended.

9.1.3 Link to Hypotheses

The GAM directly tests H2 (location-centered indicators) through the 2D spatial spline, while simultaneously controlling for H1 (host-centered indicators) via parametric terms (host_is_superhost, review_scores_rating). The model is therefore positioned as our primary tool for examining whether location effects on willingness-to-pay are non-linear and spatially clustered — a question a linear model cannot answer.

9.1.4 Methodological Choices

  • Family / Link: We use gaussian() on log(price). This stabilises variance (prices are right-skewed and heteroscedastic) and ensures consistency with the LM benchmark, which also models log(price). Coefficients can be interpreted multiplicatively: a coefficient β corresponds to a \((exp(β) − 1) × 100%\) price change.
  • Smoothing method: method = "REML" (Restricted Maximum Likelihood) is preferred over the default GCV.Cp because it is less prone to undersmoothing and provides more reliable confidence intervals.
  • Basis dimension (k): Default values are used; the spatial spline is allowed higher flexibility (EDF ≈ 28) given the geographic heterogeneity of the canton.

9.2 Model

# GAM Modell — bereinigt (Davide & Timon)

# --- 1. Data preparation ---
d.airbnb_listings_feat_gam <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  select(price, accommodates, review_scores_rating,
         number_of_reviews, longitude, latitude,
         room_type, host_is_superhost, bathrooms) %>%
  na.omit()

cat("Observations after na.omit():", nrow(d.airbnb_listings_feat_gam), "\n")
Observations after na.omit(): 3159 
# --- 2. Train/Test-Split BEFORE model fitting (prevents leakage for SVM) ---
set.seed(42)
n <- nrow(d.airbnb_listings_feat_gam)
train_idx <- sample(seq_len(n), size = floor(0.8 * n))

df_train <- d.airbnb_listings_feat_gam[ train_idx, ]
df_test  <- d.airbnb_listings_feat_gam[-train_idx, ]

cat("Train:", nrow(df_train), " | Test:", nrow(df_test), "\n")
Train: 2527  | Test: 632 
# --- 3. Fit the GAM on log(price), training data only ---
gam.main <- gam(log(price) ~
                  s(accommodates) +
                  s(review_scores_rating) +
                  s(log1p(number_of_reviews)) +
                  s(longitude, latitude) +
                  room_type +
                  host_is_superhost +
                  bathrooms,
                family = gaussian(),
                method = "REML",
                data   = df_train)

summary(gam.main)

Family: gaussian 
Link function: identity 

Formula:
log(price) ~ s(accommodates) + s(review_scores_rating) + s(log1p(number_of_reviews)) + 
    s(longitude, latitude) + room_type + host_is_superhost + 
    bathrooms

Parametric coefficients:
                      Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)            4.63771    0.02274 203.969  < 2e-16 ***
room_typeHotel room    0.64874    0.15517   4.181 3.01e-05 ***
room_typePrivate room -0.35849    0.02251 -15.926  < 2e-16 ***
room_typeShared room  -0.80666    0.10670  -7.560 5.63e-14 ***
host_is_superhostTRUE -0.01079    0.01840  -0.587    0.557    
bathrooms              0.22000    0.01512  14.554  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Approximate significance of smooth terms:
                               edf Ref.df       F  p-value    
s(accommodates)              4.577  5.567 117.850  < 2e-16 ***
s(review_scores_rating)      2.999  3.752   6.096 0.000105 ***
s(log1p(number_of_reviews))  2.634  3.290  40.071  < 2e-16 ***
s(longitude,latitude)       24.933 27.971  12.383  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

R-sq.(adj) =  0.658   Deviance explained = 66.4%
-REML = 1184.9  Scale est. = 0.14127   n = 2527
