  • ChatGPT: gam(…, method = “REML”) noch ergänzen. –> erledigt Davide
summary(d.airbnb_listings_feat_gam$price)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
   20.0    81.0   118.0   159.5   180.0  3520.0 
summary(d.airbnb_listings_feat_gam$bathrooms)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
   0.00    1.00    1.00    1.31    1.50   14.00

9.3 GAM Visualisation (DAVIDE)

# Partial effect plots—this is the main advantage of GAM over LM

# --- 4. Diagnostic plots ---
plot(gam.main,
     pages      = 1,
     shade      = TRUE,
     col        = "steelblue",
     shade.col  = "lightblue",
     seWithMean = TRUE)

edf_table <- as.data.frame(summary(gam.main)$s.table) %>% round(3)
kable(edf_table, caption = "Smooth Terms: Estimated Degrees of Freedom (EDF)")
Smooth Terms: Estimated Degrees of Freedom (EDF)
edf Ref.df F p-value
s(accommodates) 4.577 5.567 117.850 0
s(review_scores_rating) 2.999 3.752 6.096 0
s(log1p(number_of_reviews)) 2.634 3.290 40.071 0
s(longitude,latitude) 24.933 27.971 12.383 0 
cat("R-squared:         ", round(summary(gam.main)$r.sq,  3), "\n")
R-squared:          0.658 
cat("Deviance explained:", round(summary(gam.main)$dev.expl, 3), "\n")
Deviance explained: 0.664 
# --- 5. Fair LM benchmark (same target, same training data) ---
lm.gam_comparison <- lm(log(price) ~ accommodates + review_scores_rating +
                          log1p(number_of_reviews) + longitude + latitude +
                          room_type + host_is_superhost + bathrooms,
                        data = df_train)

cat("LM  R²:", round(summary(lm.gam_comparison)$r.squared, 3), "\n")
LM  R²: 0.607 
cat("GAM R²:", round(summary(gam.main)$r.sq, 3), "\n")
GAM R²: 0.658 
AIC(lm.gam_comparison, gam.main)

FEEDBACK TIMON - lm.main und gam.main haben nicht die gleiche zielvariable. ChatGPT: Euer GAM verwendet: price family = gaussian(link = “log”) Das LM verwendet: lm(log(price) ~ …) Diese Modelle sind ähnlich — aber nicht identisch. Warum das wichtig ist GAM: E(price|x) = exp(…) mit Gaussian-Fehlern auf der Response-Skala. LM: log(price) = … mit Gaussian-Fehlern auf der Log-Skala. Das sind unterschiedliche Likelihoods.

das sei methodisch heikel, aber nicht verboten. bei der interpretation muss man dabei vorsichtig sein. Lösung von ChatGPT: für das Gam den preis auch logarithmieren: gam(log(price) ~ …)

      Entscheidet euch klar zwischen:
      Variante 1
      gam(price, link="log")
      oder
      Variante 2
      gam(log(price))
      Und vergleicht danach konsistent mit LM.

ANPASSUNGEN TIMON: - ich habe beim ursprünglichen Model noch pages = 1 ergänzt um alles auf eine Seite zu bringen. sei robuster als das gelöschte: “par(mfrow = c(2,3))” - EDF > 2 habe ich in den kommentaren umgeschrieben von “# edf > 2 → clearly nonlinear” zu “# Higher EDF values indicate stronger nonlinearity”

# --- 6. Predictions on train and test (as numeric vector) ---
df_train$fitted_logprice <- as.numeric(predict(gam.main, newdata = df_train))
df_test$fitted_logprice  <- as.numeric(predict(gam.main, newdata = df_test))

# Smearing correction (Duan 1983) for log-transformed responses
sigma2   <- summary(gam.main)$scale
smearing <- exp(sigma2 / 2)

df_train$fitted_price <- exp(df_train$fitted_logprice) * smearing
df_test$fitted_price  <- exp(df_test$fitted_logprice)  * smearing

9.4 Optional: Traffic Light Map

The GAM produces a fitted price for each listing. By comparing the actual price with the GAM-recommended price, listings can be classified into three categories — providing hosts with an immediate visual cue on the regional pricing map. Choice of the ±15% Threshold The classification threshold is set to ±15% based on the residual standard deviation of the GAM on the log-scale, which corresponds to roughly exp⁡(σ)−1≈15%() - 1 % exp(σ)−1≈15%. Listings outside this band therefore deviate by more than one standard deviation from the model’s expected market price. This is a defensible, data-driven choice rather than an arbitrary cutoff.

# --- 7. Traffic-light labels for BOTH sets (same rule) ---
make_traffic_light <- function(price, fitted, threshold = 15) {
  price  <- as.numeric(price)
  fitted <- as.numeric(fitted)
  pct    <- (price - fitted) / fitted * 100
  case_when(
    pct <= -threshold ~ "Underpriced",
    pct >=  threshold ~ "Overpriced",
    TRUE              ~ "Fair Price"
  ) %>% as.factor()
}

df_train$pct_diff      <- (df_train$price - df_train$fitted_price) / df_train$fitted_price * 100
df_test$pct_diff       <- (df_test$price  - df_test$fitted_price)  / df_test$fitted_price  * 100
df_train$traffic_light <- make_traffic_light(df_train$price, df_train$fitted_price)
df_test$traffic_light  <- make_traffic_light(df_test$price,  df_test$fitted_price)

# Sanity check: distribution should be similar in train and test
cat("\nTrain:\n"); print(prop.table(table(df_train$traffic_light)))

Train:

 Fair Price  Overpriced Underpriced 
  0.3276613   0.2588049   0.4135338 
cat("\nTest:\n");  print(prop.table(table(df_test$traffic_light)))

Test:

 Fair Price  Overpriced Underpriced 
  0.3306962   0.2484177   0.4208861

9.5 Interactive Map of Canton Vaude with the GAM

# --- 8. Map of the entire dataset (descriptive view for hosts) ---
df_full <- bind_rows(df_train, df_test)

color_map <- c("Underpriced" = "#EF9F27",
               "Fair Price"  = "#1D9E75",
               "Overpriced"  = "#D85A30")
df_full$marker_color <- color_map[as.character(df_full$traffic_light)]

leaflet() %>%
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) %>%
  setView(lng = 6.65, lat = 46.52, zoom = 9) %>%
  addCircleMarkers(
    data = df_full,
    lng = ~longitude, lat = ~latitude,
    radius = 4, fillColor = ~marker_color,
    fillOpacity = 0.85, stroke = FALSE,
    popup = ~paste0(
      "<b>Host Verdict:</b> ", traffic_light, "<br>",
      "<b>Actual Price:</b> CHF ", round(price), "<br>",
      "<b>GAM Recommended:</b> CHF ", round(fitted_price), "<br>",
      "<b>Difference:</b> ", round(pct_diff, 1), "%"
    )
  ) %>%
  addLegend(
    position = "bottomright",
    colors   = color_map,
    labels   = c("Underpriced (Increase)",
                 "Fair Price (±15%)",
                 "Overpriced (Decrease)"),
    title    = "Pricing Strategy vs. Market (GAM)",
    opacity  = 0.85
  )
# Note: df_train and df_test are passed on to the SVM block.

FEEDBACK TIMON - allenfalls muss man hier darauf hinweisen, dass die 15% subjektiv gewählt sind. allenfalls finden wir dazu noch etwas. - ALLGEMEINE FRAGE: haben wir hier ein target-leakage für die SVM? müssen wir die Trainingsdaten und die TEstdaten bereits vor dem definieren der Kategorien definieren damit wir nicht einen Bias drin haben? allenfalls müssen wir das noch besprechen. ChatGPT: Saubere Lösung Idealerweise: Train/Test Split GAM nur auf Train fitten Labels erzeugen SVM trainieren Test separat evaluieren

9.6 Interpretation

Model Overview The GAM was fitted on [NEU EINFÜGEN: n_train] training observations (80% split) using gaussian() family on log(price) with REML smoothing. The model explains approximately [NEU EINFÜGEN: dev.expl]% of deviance (adjusted $R^2 = $ [NEU EINFÜGEN]), which is strong performance for hedonic price modelling in the short-term rental market and clearly outperforms the LM benchmark on the same training data. Parametric Terms (Linear Effects) Room Type is the strongest categorical price driver in the model. Using Entire home/apt as the reference category:

Hotel room: significant positive premium ($= $ [NEU], p < 0.001). On the multiplicative scale, hotel rooms command roughly [NEU: exp(β)]× the price of a comparable entire home. Private room: significant negative effect ($= $ [NEU], p < 0.001), corresponding to approximately a [NEU: (1-exp(β))*100]% price reduction. Shared room: not statistically significant, likely due to the small number of shared room listings in Vaud.

Bathrooms show a highly significant positive effect ($= $ [NEU], p < 0.001). Each additional bathroom is associated with approximately a [NEU: (exp(β)-1)*100]% price increase, reflecting the importance of physical listing characteristics for guest willingness to pay. Superhost status shows a small, marginally significant coefficient ($= $ [NEU], p ≈ [NEU]). This finding is consistent across all models in this study — the GLM Binomial, the Neural Network, and the GAM all converge on the same conclusion: superhost status does not yield a measurable price premium. Smooth Terms (Non-linear Effects) All four smooth terms are highly significant (p < 2e-16) and show substantial non-linearity, as indicated by their EDF values. An EDF close to 1 would indicate a near-linear relationship, making a spline unnecessary. All EDF values here are far above 1, confirming that non-linear smoothing is statistically justified for every continuous predictor. The 2D spatial spline s(longitude, latitude) deserves particular attention with an EDF of approximately [NEU: ca. 27-28]. This indicates a highly complex spatial price surface across the Canton of Vaud — locations closer to Lake Geneva and urban centres such as Lausanne command significantly higher prices, while rural and alpine areas show distinct pricing patterns. This spatial component alone justifies the GAM approach for a regionally scoped pricing tool and provides direct empirical support for hypothesis H2 (location-centered indicators drive willingness-to-pay). Cross-Model Comparison: Superhost Effect One of the most consistent findings across all models in this study is the absence of a superhost price premium. This convergence across fundamentally different model architectures — parametric (LM), non-parametric (GAM), and connectionist (Neural Network) — constitutes strong evidence against a price-based superhost effect. Superhosts do not appear to charge higher prices; rather, as Pascal’s Quasi-Poisson model demonstrates, they achieve 2.6× higher occupancy through competitive pricing. The data therefore suggest a volume-over-margin strategy among superhost-status hosts in Canton Vaud. Business Case Implications The GAM results provide three actionable insights for the pricing decision-support tool:

Physical listing characteristics (room type, bathroom count, guest capacity) are the primary price drivers a host can directly control. The model recommends prioritising these structural factors in the pricing interface. Location matters non-linearly. The spatial spline captures granular geographic price gradients that a postcode or neighbourhood dummy variable would miss. This validates the use of coordinates as a continuous input in the recommendation tool. Pursuing superhost status should be framed as an occupancy strategy, not a pricing strategy. Hosts seeking the superhost badge are advised to price competitively to accumulate bookings and reviews, with the understanding that revenue gains come from higher occupancy rather than a higher nightly rate.

9.7 1.5 Limitations

Despite its strong performance, the GAM has several limitations that should be acknowledged when interpreting the results and using the pricing tool: 1. Cross-sectional snapshot, no temporal dynamics. The model is trained on a single point in time and ignores seasonality, weekday effects, and event-driven demand spikes (e.g. Montreux Jazz Festival, ski season). Real Airbnb pricing is highly time-dependent — our recommendations therefore represent an “average market price” rather than a dynamic optimum. 2. Endogeneity of the price signal. We treat the listed price as a proxy for willingness-to-pay, but listed prices reflect what hosts charge, not necessarily what guests would pay. Listings that remain unbooked at their current price are still included in the training set, which may bias the model towards optimistic price levels. 3. Smearing correction assumes homoscedastic residuals. The bias correction applied when transforming log(price) predictions back to the price scale assumes constant variance across the prediction range. If residual variance is heteroscedastic — which is plausible for very high-end or very low-end listings — back-transformed prices may be slightly biased at the tails. 4. Subjective ±15% threshold for the traffic light. While the threshold is anchored in the residual standard deviation of the GAM, it remains a discretionary choice. A more rigorous approach would use prediction intervals from the GAM directly, or define the bands using quantiles of the empirical residual distribution. 5. Missing predictors. Several variables likely to affect price are not in the dataset: amenity counts (pool, parking, Wi-Fi quality), photo quality, listing description sentiment, exact distance to public transport, and noise/view characteristics. The unexplained [NEU: 100 - dev.expl]% of deviance is partly attributable to these omitted variables. 6. Spatial spline can mask underlying causes. While s(longitude, latitude) fits the spatial price pattern very well, it does not explain why certain locations are more expensive. For business purposes the surface is sufficient, but for causal inference (e.g. “does proximity to the lake cause higher prices, or is it correlated with property quality?”), the spline alone is insufficient. 7. Non-extrapolation outside the training region. The 2D spatial spline is reliable only within the convex hull of observed listings. Predictions for new listings in under-sampled areas (remote alpine villages, isolated communes) carry substantially higher uncertainty than the standard errors suggest.

10 SVM (TIMON - Davide reviewed)

10.1 Theory

10.2 Model

# Setting a seed ensures reproducibility. 
set.seed(42) 

# Define the relevant columns for the SVM model.
# We include 'price' because the use case requires evaluating if a 
# *given* price for a specific set of features is Overpriced, Fair, or Underpriced.
svm.coloums <- c("traffic_light", "price", "accommodates", "review_scores_rating", 
                 "number_of_reviews", "room_type", "host_is_superhost")

# Creating the 80% Training and 20% Test datasets
d.svm_train <- df_train[, svm.coloums]
d.svm_test  <- df_test[, svm.coloums]

# Verifying the split visually
cat("Training set size:", nrow(d.svm_train), "\n")
Training set size: 2527 
cat("Test set size:", nrow(d.svm_test), "\n")
Test set size: 632 
# We use 10-fold cross-validation to find the optimal 'cost' and 'gamma' parameters 
# for the radial basis function (RBF) kernel.

tuned_svm <- tune(
  svm,
  traffic_light ~ ., # 'traffic_light' was already converted to a factor in the GAM step
  data = d.svm_train,
  kernel = "radial",
  ranges = list(
    cost = 2^(-6:10),
    gamma = 2^(-5:2)
  )
)

# Extracting the best parameters found during cross-validation
svm.model_cost <- as.numeric(tuned_svm$best.parameters["cost"])
svm.model_gamma <- as.numeric(tuned_svm$best.parameters["gamma"])


#displaying Parameters for visual confirmation
cat("Optimal Cost:", svm.model_cost, "\n")
Optimal Cost: 256 
cat("Optimal Gamma:", svm.model_gamma, "\n")
Optimal Gamma: 0.0625 
cat("Best Cross-Validation Performance (Error Rate):", round(tuned_svm$best.performance, 4), "\n")
Best Cross-Validation Performance (Error Rate): 0.2533 
# --- Final SVM Model & Evaluation ---

# Training the final Support Vector Machine using the optimized parameters
svm.main <- svm(traffic_light ~ ., 
                data = d.svm_train, 
                kernel = "radial", 
                cost = svm.model_cost, 
                gamma = svm.model_gamma, 
                scale = TRUE) # Scaling to treat all features equally

# Predicting the traffic light categories on test-data
svm.test_pred <- predict(svm.main, d.svm_test)

# Generating the Confusion Matrix to evaluate classification performance
svm.conf_matrix <- table(Predicted = svm.test_pred, Actual = d.svm_test$traffic_light)
print("Confusion Matrix:")
[1] "Confusion Matrix:"
print(svm.conf_matrix)
             Actual
Predicted     Fair Price Overpriced Underpriced
  Fair Price         121         36          45
  Overpriced          27        114           1
  Underpriced         61          7         220
# Calculating overall Accuracy
svm_accuracy <- sum(diag(svm.conf_matrix)) / sum(svm.conf_matrix)
cat("\nSVM Test Accuracy:", round(svm_accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")

SVM Test Accuracy: 71.99 %

10.3 Crossvalidation

svm.cv1 <- svm(traffic_light ~ ., 
                data = d.svm_train, 
                kernel = "linear", 
                cost = svm.model_cost, 
                gamma = svm.model_gamma, 
                scale = TRUE) # Scaling to treat all features equally

# Predicting the traffic light categories on test-data
svm.test_pred_cv1 <- predict(svm.cv1, d.svm_test)

# Generating the Confusion Matrix to evaluate classification performance
svm.conf_matrix_cv1 <- table(Predicted = svm.test_pred_cv1, Actual = d.svm_test$traffic_light)
print("Confusion Matrix:")
[1] "Confusion Matrix:"
print(svm.conf_matrix_cv1)
             Actual
Predicted     Fair Price Overpriced Underpriced
  Fair Price         113         45          43
  Overpriced          28        109           5
  Underpriced         68          3         218
# Calculating overall Accuracy
svm_accuracy_cv1 <- sum(diag(svm.conf_matrix_cv1)) / sum(svm.conf_matrix_cv1)
cat("\nSVM Test Accuracy:", round(svm_accuracy_cv1 * 100, 2), "%\n")

SVM Test Accuracy: 69.62 %
svm.cv2 <- svm(traffic_light ~ ., 
                data = d.svm_train, 
                kernel = "polynomial", 
                cost = svm.model_cost, 
                gamma = svm.model_gamma, 
                scale = TRUE) # Scaling to treat all features equally

# Predicting the traffic light categories on test-data
svm.test_pred_cv2 <- predict(svm.cv2, d.svm_test)

# Generating the Confusion Matrix to evaluate classification performance
svm.conf_matrix_cv2 <- table(Predicted = svm.test_pred_cv2, Actual = d.svm_test$traffic_light)
print("Confusion Matrix:")
[1] "Confusion Matrix:"
print(svm.conf_matrix_cv2)
             Actual
Predicted     Fair Price Overpriced Underpriced
  Fair Price         124         42          48
  Overpriced          26        113           3
  Underpriced         59          2         215
# Calculating overall Accuracy
svm_accuracy_cv2 <- sum(diag(svm.conf_matrix_cv2)) / sum(svm.conf_matrix_cv2)
cat("\nSVM Test Accuracy:", round(svm_accuracy_cv2 * 100, 2), "%\n")

SVM Test Accuracy: 71.52 %
svm.cv3 <- svm(traffic_light ~ ., 
                data = d.svm_train, 
                kernel = "radial", 
                cost = 128, 
                gamma = 0.5, 
                scale = TRUE) # Scaling to treat all features equally

# Predicting the traffic light categories on test-data
svm.test_pred_cv3 <- predict(svm.cv3, d.svm_test)

# Generating the Confusion Matrix to evaluate classification performance
svm.conf_matrix_cv3 <- table(Predicted = svm.test_pred_cv3, Actual = d.svm_test$traffic_light)
print("Confusion Matrix:")
[1] "Confusion Matrix:"
print(svm.conf_matrix_cv3)
             Actual
Predicted     Fair Price Overpriced Underpriced
  Fair Price         118         40          47
  Overpriced          30        110           3
  Underpriced         61          7         216
# Calculating overall Accuracy
svm_accuracy_cv3 <- sum(diag(svm.conf_matrix_cv3)) / sum(svm.conf_matrix_cv3)
cat("\nSVM Test Accuracy:", round(svm_accuracy_cv3 * 100, 2), "%\n")

SVM Test Accuracy: 70.25 %

10.4 Interpretion

Support Vector Machine (SVM) Classification To operationalize our pricing decision-support system, we trained a Support Vector Machine (SVM) using a radial basis function (RBF) kernel. The objective of this model is to classify whether a host’s proposed listing strategy is “Underpriced,” a “Fair Price,” or “Overpriced.”

We established the ground truth labels for these three categories based on the percentage deviation from our GAM’s expected market price. The data was split into an 80% training set and a 20% test set. To ensure optimal model performance, we utilized 10-fold cross-validation to tune the hyperparameters, systematically searching across a grid of cost and gamma values.

By feeding the SVM both the listing characteristics (e.g., accommodates, room type, superhost status) and the specific price the host intends to charge, the model learns the non-linear boundaries between our three pricing tiers. When evaluated on the unseen test dataset, the confusion matrix reveals how well the algorithm generalized, providing a concrete accuracy metric that validates the reliability of our traffic-light recommendation tool.

Using the variables contained in the dataset and some targeted tuning, we’ve managed to achieve an accuracy of 62%. The model’s main challenge lies in determining whether a price is fair or not, where it only performed little better than randomness. This is evident not only in the “Fair Price” category but also in the other two, where the largest group of incorrect predictions is found in the “Fair Price” group.

However, the data itself represents a clear shortcoming for the model. Prediction is virtually impossible without operationalizing the location. While we have the coordinates, these alone are hardly meaningful. For example, the distance to city centers or landmarks would be important to enable better categorization.

11 ANN (DAVIDE) Identisch mit Pascal

11.1 Theory

11.2 Model

# --- ANN: Model 1 (Naive Approach) ---
# Selecting basic variables. We omit missing values for this simplified approach.
d.ann_naive <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  select(
    price,                  
    review_scores_rating,   
    number_of_reviews,      
    host_is_superhost,      
    accommodates            
  ) %>%
  na.omit() 

# Neural Networks perform best (and converge faster) when all input features 
# are scaled to a similar range, typically [0, 1].
normalize <- function(x) {
  return ((x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)))
}

# Apply min-max normalization to all columns
d.ann_naive_scaled <- as.data.frame(lapply(d.ann_naive, normalize))

# Train the basic Neural Network
# We use 1 hidden layer with 3 neurons. linear.output = TRUE is used for regression (predicting a continuous price).
set.seed(42) 
nn_naive <- neuralnet(
  price ~ review_scores_rating + number_of_reviews + host_is_superhost + accommodates,
  data = d.ann_naive_scaled,
  hidden = 3,
  linear.output = TRUE,
  stepmax = 1e6         
)

# Visualize the network architecture
plot(nn_naive)

# --- Testing Hypothesis 3 (H3) with the Naive Model ---
# Calculate the mean for our features to represent a "statistically average" Airbnb
mean_rating <- mean(d.ann_naive_scaled$review_scores_rating)
mean_reviews <- mean(d.ann_naive_scaled$number_of_reviews)
mean_acc <- mean(d.ann_naive_scaled$accommodates)

# Create two identical fictional hosts: one is a regular host (0), one is a Superhost (1)
test_data_h3_naive <- data.frame(
  review_scores_rating = c(mean_rating, mean_rating),
  number_of_reviews = c(mean_reviews, mean_reviews),
  host_is_superhost = c(0, 1), # The only difference between the two instances
  accommodates = c(mean_acc, mean_acc)
)

# Predict the scaled prices
predictions_h3_naive <- compute(nn_naive, test_data_h3_naive)

# Reverse the min-max scaling to interpret the results in real CHF
min_price <- min(d.ann_naive$price)
max_price <- max(d.ann_naive$price)
real_prices_naive <- predictions_h3_naive$net.result * (max_price - min_price) + min_price

print("Predicted Prices (Naive Model): Regular Host vs. Superhost")
[1] "Predicted Prices (Naive Model): Regular Host vs. Superhost"
print(real_prices_naive)
         [,1]
[1,] 160.1493
[2,] 131.6514
# --- ANN: Model 2 (Expanded Model handling Omitted Variable Bias) ---

# Data Preparation & Dimensionality Reduction
d_model_ready <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  select(
    price,                  
    review_scores_rating,   
    number_of_reviews,      
    host_is_superhost,      
    accommodates,
    property_type,          
    room_type               
  ) %>%
  na.omit() %>%
  # put rare property types into an "Other" category to keep the variable tidy
  mutate(property_type = fct_lump_n(property_type, n = 4, other_level = "Other"))

# One-Hot Encoding: We convert categorical variables into binary dummy variables.
d_dummies <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(~ . - 1, data = d_model_ready))

# Sanitize column names to prevent crashes
colnames(d_dummies) <- make.names(colnames(d_dummies))

d_scaled <- as.data.frame(lapply(d_dummies, normalize))

set.seed(42) 
nn_expanded <- neuralnet(
  price ~ ., 
  data = d_scaled,
  hidden = 3,
  linear.output = TRUE, 
  stepmax = 1e6         
)

plot(nn_expanded)

# --- 5. Prediction & Test for H3 ---
# To conduct a fair "what-if" analysis (Partial Dependence), we take the mean of ALL 
# variables to represent an absolutely "average" listing.
test_data_h3_exp <- as.data.frame(lapply(d_scaled, mean))

# Duplicate the row to create two identical hypothetical listings
test_data_h3_exp <- rbind(test_data_h3_exp, test_data_h3_exp)

# Isolate the Superhost effect: Row 1 = Regular Host, Row 2 = Superhost
test_data_h3_exp$host_is_superhostFALSE <- c(1, 0) 
test_data_h3_exp$host_is_superhostTRUE <- c(0, 1)

# Predict prices (excluding the 'price' column from the input)
predictions_h3_exp <- compute(nn_expanded, test_data_h3_exp %>% select(-price))

# Reverse scaling using the min/max from the unscaled dataset
min_price_exp <- min(d_model_ready$price)
max_price_exp <- max(d_model_ready$price)

real_prices_expanded <- predictions_h3_exp$net.result * (max_price_exp - min_price_exp) + min_price_exp

print("Predicted Prices (Expanded Model): Regular Host vs. Superhost")
[1] "Predicted Prices (Expanded Model): Regular Host vs. Superhost"
print(real_prices_expanded)
         [,1]
[1,] 493.6510
[2,] 493.3796

11.3 Interpretation ANN

Context and Hypotheses The objective of this analysis was to develop a data-driven pricing strategy for Airbnb hosts in the Canton of Vaud. The focus was on identifying the factors that influence a guest’s Willingness to Pay (WTP) and how hosts can adjust their pricing accordingly. A central hypothesis (H3) posited that a host’s “Superhost” status justifies a significant price premium, as it signals higher trust and superior service quality. The actual listed prices were used as a proxy for WTP.

Model 1: The Naive Neural Network (Omitted Variable Bias) In an initial, simplified approach, an Artificial Neural Network (Multilayer Perceptron) was trained using only four features: review score, number of reviews, superhost status, and listing capacity (accommodates), predicting the target variable (price).

Model 1 Result: Contrary to expectations, the naive network predicted a lower price for a Superhost (approx. 132 CHF) compared to a regular host (approx. 160 CHF).

Interpretation: This result is a textbook example of “Omitted Variable Bias.” In practice, hosts often achieve Superhost status through high occupancy rates, which are frequently driven by highly competitive, lower-than-average pricing. Furthermore, this naive model lacked critical contextual information, such as property and room types. Extremely expensive luxury listings, for instance, are often managed by professional agencies that may not hold an individual Superhost badge, heavily skewing the data.

Model 2: The Expanded Model (One-Hot Encoding) To correct this bias, the model was expanded in a second iteration to include structural categorical variables: room_type (e.g., Private room vs. Entire home) and property_type (e.g., Apartment vs. Chalet). Because Neural Networks require purely numerical inputs, these categories were transformed into binary dummy variables using One-Hot Encoding.

Model 2 Result: When testing a fictional, perfectly “average” listing (holding room type, capacity, and all other variables constant), the predicted price difference was marginal: 483.29 CHF (Regular Host) compared to 482.64 CHF (Superhost).

Interpretation: Once we utilize dummy variables to ensure an “apples-to-apples” comparison, the supposed price penalty for Superhosts almost entirely disappears. Simultaneously, this refutes our initial Hypothesis (H3): the Superhost status alone does not command a significantly higher willingness to pay from guests.

Business Case Conclusion For our Pricing Decision-Support System, this implies that price recommendations must be anchored primarily in structural features (capacity, location, property type). While striving for Superhost status remains highly advisable from a business perspective to increase listing visibility and overall booking volume (Occupancy), it does not inherently justify a noticeable markup on the nightly market rate.

12 Results

13 Conclusion

14 Limitations

15 Use of GenAI

16 Zum löschen:

17 ANN (PASCAL)

17.1 Theory

17.2 Model

d.airbnb_listings_feat_ANN <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  select(
    price,                  
    review_scores_rating,   
    number_of_reviews,      
    host_is_superhost,      
    accommodates            
  ) %>%
  na.omit() # Für diesen simplen Ansatz werfen wir fehlende Werte einfach raus

# 3. 
# Neuronale Netze funktionieren am besten, wenn alle Werte zwischen 0 und 1 liegen.
normalize <- function(x) {
  return ((x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)))
}
d.airbnb_listings_feat_ANN_scaled <- as.data.frame(lapply(d.airbnb_listings_feat_ANN, normalize))

# 4. Das Neuronale Netz trainieren
# Wir nutzen 1 Hidden Layer mit 3 Neuronen (sehr simpel)
set.seed(42) # Für reproduzierbare Ergebnisse
nn_simple <- neuralnet(
  price ~ review_scores_rating + number_of_reviews + host_is_superhost + accommodates,
  data = d.airbnb_listings_feat_ANN_scaled,
  hidden = 3,
  linear.output = TRUE,
  stepmax = 1e6         
)

# 5. Das Netz visualisieren
plot(nn_simple)


# Durchschnittswerte für unser fiktives Airbnb berechnen
mean_rating <- mean(d.airbnb_listings_feat_ANN_scaled$review_scores_rating)
mean_reviews <- mean(d.airbnb_listings_feat_ANN_scaled$number_of_reviews)
mean_acc <- mean(d.airbnb_listings_feat_ANN_scaled$accommodates)

# Test für H3: Macht Superhost einen Unterschied?
# Wir erfinden zwei Hosts: Exakt gleich, nur einer ist Superhost (1), der andere nicht (0)
test_data_h3 <- data.frame(
  review_scores_rating = c(mean_rating, mean_rating),
  number_of_reviews = c(mean_reviews, mean_reviews),
  host_is_superhost = c(0, 1), # <-- Hier Unterschied!
  accommodates = c(mean_acc, mean_acc)
)

# Das Netz den Preis vorhersagen lassen
predictions_h3 <- compute(nn_simple, test_data_h3)

# Skalierung rückgängig machen, um echte CHF-Preise zu sehen
min_price <- min(d.airbnb_listings_feat_ANN$price)
max_price <- max(d.airbnb_listings_feat_ANN$price)
echte_preise <- predictions_h3$net.result * (max_price - min_price) + min_price

print(echte_preise)
         [,1]
[1,] 160.1493
[2,] 131.6514
# --- 1. Datenaufbereitung & Reduzierung ---
d_model_ready <- d.airbnb_listings_feat %>%
  select(
    price,                  
    review_scores_rating,   
    number_of_reviews,      
    host_is_superhost,      
    accommodates,
    property_type,          
    room_type               
  ) %>%
  na.omit() %>%
  mutate(property_type = fct_lump_n(property_type, n = 4, other_level = "Other"))

# --- 2. Dummy-Variablen erstellen (One-Hot-Encoding) ---

d_dummies <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(~ . - 1, data = d_model_ready))

# Leer- oder Sonderzeichen in Spaltennamen bringen neuralnet zum Absturz. 
colnames(d_dummies) <- make.names(colnames(d_dummies))


# --- 3. Skalierung ---
normalize <- function(x) {
  return ((x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)))
}
# Wir wenden normalize auf alle Spalten an. DummyVariabeln bleiben gelich
d_scaled <- as.data.frame(lapply(d_dummies, normalize))


# --- 4. Das Neuronale Netz trainieren ---
set.seed(42) 


nn_simple <- neuralnet(
  price ~ .,
  data = d_scaled,
  hidden = 3,
  linear.output = TRUE, 
  stepmax = 1e6         
)

# 5. Das Netz visualisieren
plot(nn_simple)


# --- 6. Vorhersage & Test für H3 ---
# Um eine faire "Was-wäre-wenn"-Analyse zu machen, nehmen wir einfach von ALLEN 
# Variablen im Datensatz den Durchschnitt (das repräsentiert das "durchschnittliche Airbnb").
test_data_h3 <- as.data.frame(lapply(d_scaled, mean))
# Wir verdoppeln diese Zeile, damit wir zwei identische "durchschnittliche" Hosts haben
test_data_h3 <- rbind(test_data_h3, test_data_h3)

# Jetzt verändern wir NUR den Superhost-Status:
# Zeile 1 = Kein Superhost (0), Zeile 2 = Superhost (1)
test_data_h3$host_is_superhostFALSE <- c(1, 0) 
test_data_h3$host_is_superhostTRUE <- c(0, 1)

# Das Netz den Preis vorhersagen lassen (Wir schließen die Preis-Spalte für den Test aus)
predictions_h3 <- compute(nn_simple, test_data_h3 %>% select(-price))

# Skalierung rückgängig machen mit den Werten aus d_model_ready (Originaldaten!)
min_price <- min(d_model_ready$price)
max_price <- max(d_model_ready$price)

echte_preise <- predictions_h3$net.result * (max_price - min_price) + min_price

print(echte_preise)
         [,1]
[1,] 493.6510
[2,] 493.3796

17.3 Interpretation ANN

Ausgangslage und Hypothesen Ziel der Untersuchung war es, eine datengetriebene Pricing-Strategie für Airbnb-Hosts im Kanton Waadt (Vaud) zu entwickeln. Im Fokus stand die Frage, welche Faktoren die Zahlungsbereitschaft (Willingness to Pay, WTP) der Gäste beeinflussen und wie Hosts ihre Preise entsprechend anpassen können. Eine zentrale Hypothese (H3) nahm an, dass der “Superhost”-Status eines Vermieters einen signifikanten Preisaufschlag rechtfertigt, da er höheres Vertrauen und eine bessere Servicequalität suggeriert. Als Proxy für die WTP wurden die tatsächlich aufgerufenen Preise der Inserate verwendet.

Modell 1: Das naive Neuronale Netz (Omitted Variable Bias) In einem ersten, vereinfachten Ansatz wurde ein Neuronales Netz (Multilayer Perceptron) mit nur vier Variablen trainiert: Preis (Zielvariable), Bewertung (Review Score), Anzahl der Bewertungen und Superhost-Status (ja/nein), kontrolliert durch die maximale Gästeanzahl (accommodates).

Ergebnis Modell 1: Entgegen der Erwartungen sagte das Netz für einen Superhost einen niedrigeren Preis (ca. 132 CHF) voraus als für einen regulären Host (ca. 160 CHF).

Interpretation: Dieses Ergebnis ist ein klassischer Fall von “Omitted Variable Bias” (Verzerrung durch weggelassene Variablen). Superhosts erzielen ihren Status oft durch hohe Auslastung (viele Buchungen), was in der Praxis häufig über wettbewerbsfähige, tendenziell niedrigere Preise erreicht wird. Zudem fehlen in diesem Modell entscheidende Kontextinformationen wie die Art der Unterkunft: Luxuriöse, sehr teure Objekte werden oft von professionellen Agenturen verwaltet, die seltener über einen individuellen Superhost-Badge verfügen.

Modell 2: Das erweiterte Modell (One-Hot-Encoding) Um diese Verzerrung zu korrigieren, wurde das Modell im zweiten Schritt um kategoriale Variablen erweitert: die Art der Unterkunft (room_type, z.B. Privatzimmer vs. ganze Wohnung) und den Immobilientyp (property_type, z.B. Apartment vs. Chalet). Da Neuronale Netze nur numerische Inputs verarbeiten können, wurden diese Kategorien mittels “One-Hot-Encoding” in binäre Dummy-Variablen übersetzt.

Ergebnis Modell 2: Beim Test eines fiktiven, absolut durchschnittlichen Inserats (gleicher Raumtyp, gleiche Kapazität etc.) unterschied sich der Preis nur noch marginal: 483,29 CHF (kein Superhost) zu 482,64 CHF (Superhost).

Interpretation: Wenn wir durch Dummy-Variablen sicherstellen, dass wir “Äpfel mit Äpfeln” vergleichen, verschwindet der vermeintliche Preisabschlag für Superhosts fast vollständig. Gleichzeitig wird unsere Ausgangshypothese (H3) widerlegt: Der reine Superhost-Status führt nicht zu einer signifikant höheren Zahlungsbereitschaft der Gäste.

Fazit für den Business Case Für das Decision-Support-System bedeutet dies, dass Pricing-Empfehlungen primär auf strukturellen Faktoren (Größe, Lage, Art der Immobilie) basieren müssen. Sich den Superhost-Status zu erarbeiten, ist für Hosts aus betriebswirtschaftlicher Sicht zwar ratsam, um die Sichtbarkeit und Buchungswahrscheinlichkeit (Occupancy) zu erhöhen, rechtfertigt aber per se keinen spürbaren Preisaufschlag im Markt.

17.4 GLM Quasi-Poisson Results for Businesscase

# --- GLM Quasi-Poisson Results Extraction (Pascal) ---
# Extract model results into a clean dataframe using broom::tidy()
d.results_glm_quasipoisson <- tidy(glm_quasipoisson) %>%
  # Calculate the percentage change for easier business interpretation.
  # Since Poisson models use a log-link function, we must exponentiate the coefficients (estimates) 
  # to interpret them as multiplicative effects on the expected count (occupancy).
  mutate(
    pct_change = (exp(estimate) - 1) * 100,
    # Round the percentage change to 2 decimal places for readability
    pct_change = round(pct_change, 2)
  ) %>%
  # Select only the columns most relevant for communicating insights to the client
  select(term, estimate, pct_change, p.value)

print(d.results_glm_quasipoisson)
# A tibble: 8 × 4
  term                  estimate pct_change   p.value
  <chr>                    <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>
1 (Intercept)            2.80       1550.   2.81e- 14
2 price                 -0.00306      -0.31 9.64e- 22
3 host_is_superhostTRUE  0.957       160.   2.30e-102
4 review_scores_rating   0.226        25.4  3.52e-  3
5 accommodates           0.0383        3.91 1.62e-  3
6 room_typeHotel room    0.553        73.8  8.73e-  2
7 room_typePrivate room -0.136       -12.8  1.07e-  2
8 room_typeShared room  -0.188       -17.2  5.29e-  1
